title: Throwing a porcelain tea cup
subtitle: A delicate wheel-thrown cup in porcelain with thin pulled walls
excerpt: A wheel-thrown tea cup in porcelain with walls pulled to 3-4 mm and a graceful foot ring. Porcelain is the most demanding throwing body: its low plasticity means it collapses at slight overwork. This tutorial covers the modified throwing sequence that manages porcelain's narrower working window. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln access.
sourceNotes: Porcelain throwing technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911), porcelain entry, for historical context.

glossaryTerms.definition: A translucent, white-firing high-temperature clay body. Fires to maturity at cone 10 (1280°C). Very low plasticity compared with stoneware; it collapses faster at the wheel.
Settling a clay mound on the wheel head so it rotates without wobbling. The foundation of all wheel work.
Pressing a hole into the centre of a centred mound to start a thrown pot.
Drawing clay upward between fingers to thin and raise the wall of a thrown pot.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim.
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on, carved at the leather-hard stage.
The first firing, around cone 06 (1000°C), that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware.
A pyrometric measure of heat-work in the kiln. Cone 06 is roughly 1000°C; cone 6 is roughly 1220°C; cone 10 is roughly 1280°C.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors. Greenware sanding happens outdoors with a mask and never in a kitchen-air-shared space.

Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Wear welder shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area.
Porcelain is the glass of the clay world: beautiful, white, translucent when thinned to 3-4 mm, and unforgiving at the wheel. Its low plasticity means that a stoneware centring technique applied to
porcelain

collapses the wall within one or two additional pulls. This cup is thrown from 250 g of porcelain. A kiln and wheel are required. The finished cup fires to approximately 85 mm diameter by 70 mm tall, with walls 3-4 mm thick.

items.name: Porcelain
items.substitution: Porcelain-grogged for more forgiving walls at the cost of some translucency

content.text: Preparation
Wedge the porcelain thoroughly. Porcelain air bubbles cause blowouts in the kiln. Spiral wedge or ram-head wedge until the cut-through surface shows no visible bubbles.
Centring
porcelain requires a faster wheel speed and lighter hands than stoneware. The clay is less plastic and responds more to friction than pressure.
Centring

Attach the 250 g ball firmly to the bat. Run the wheel at medium-high speed. Use both hands to centre the mound, keeping hands wet and pressure even. Porcelain slides under too much pressure; centre with a light downward squeeze from both palms rather than pushing from one side. The mound is centred when it runs still under a fixed fingertip.
Opening
Apply
opening

with the dominant thumb pressing straight down into the centre. Stop 6-8 mm from the bat: this becomes the base thickness. Porcelain bases thinner than 5 mm crack under their own weight during drying. Open to about 50 mm in diameter for this cup size.
Pulling the walls
Porcelain cups need two pulls at most, sometimes one. Each pull removes clay at the base of the wall and transfers it upward. For
pulling a wall

in porcelain, place the inside fingers at the base of the wall and the outside fingers opposite. Apply a light lifting pressure while moving both hands upward at the same speed as the wall rises. The pull should take 3-4 seconds per revolution. Do not add extra water mid-pull; porcelain saturates quickly and loses structure.

After the first pull the wall should be around 6-7 mm thick. The second pull brings it to 3-4 mm. If the rim wobbles after the second pull, do not attempt to straighten it by pushing. Let the cup stiffen on the bat for 10-15 minutes, then use the needle tool to cut a level rim before continuing.
Finishing the rim

Compress the rim by folding the chamois over it and drawing it gently around as the wheel turns slowly. A compressed rim is thicker and more even than an un-compressed one. Porcelain rims are prone to warping in the bisque firing if they are too thin or uneven.
Drying, trimming, and firing
Dry the cup under plastic for the first 12 hours so the rim and base dry at the same rate. When
leather-hard
, turn the cup and trim a
foot ring
with the loop tool. The foot ring on a porcelain cup is delicate: 3-4 mm wide and 5-6 mm tall. Load the kiln once bone-dry.
Bisque
at
cone
06, then glaze and fire to cone 10 for mature porcelain.
Care

Cone 10 porcelain with a food-safe glaze is suitable for daily use. Dishwasher-safe once the glaze has been verified as non-crazing. The thin walls make the cup more fragile than a stoneware cup; handle with care when washing by hand.
Variations
Add a pulled handle at the leather-hard stage for a handled tea cup shape.
Throw a saucer from a second 150 g ball to match; centre and open to a wide shallow form.
Apply a celadon glaze for the classic translucent blue-green finish on white porcelain.

rows.cause: Too much water, or the pull speed was too slow
rows.fix: Remove excess water from the inside with the sponge before pulling. Pull the wall at a steady pace without pausing mid-pull.

rows.problem: Rim warps in the bisque firing
rows.cause: Rim was too thin or unevenly compressed before drying
rows.fix: Compress the rim with the chamois after each pull. Dry the cup slowly under plastic.

rows.problem: Cup cracks at the base after throwing
rows.cause: Base was too thin, or the bat was wet when the cup was set to dry
rows.fix: Leave at least 6 mm of clay at the base. Set the cup on a dry plaster bat or a board to dry.
